questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Determine the dimensions of angular acceleration $\alpha$.
Some of the equations involving related quantities are
$\omega = \dfrac{\theta_2 - \theta_1}{t_2 - t_1}$, $\alpha = \dfrac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{t_2 - t_1}$, $\Gamma = F \cdot r$ and $I = mr^2$.
The symbols have standard meanings.
questions.solution:
From the given equations, the angular velocity $\omega$ is given by $\omega = \dfrac{\theta_2 - \theta_1}{t_2 - t_1}$.

Since angular displacement $\theta$ is a dimensionless quantity, its dimensions are $[M^0 L^0 T^0]$.

The dimensions of time $t$ are $[T]$.

Thus, the dimensions of angular velocity $\omega$ are $\dfrac{[M^0 L^0 T^0]}{[T]} = [T^{-1}]$.

The angular acceleration $\alpha$ is given by $\alpha = \dfrac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{t_2 - t_1}$.

Substituting the dimensions of $\omega$ and $t$, the dimensions of $\alpha$ are $\dfrac{[T^{-1}]}{[T]} = [T^{-2}]$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the dimensions of frequency.
questions.solution:
Frequency is defined as the number of cycles per unit time.

The formula for frequency is $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$, where $T$ is the time period.

Therefore, the dimensional formula of frequency is $[M^{0}L^{0}T^{-1}]$ or simply $T^{-1}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the dimensions of the electric field $E$. The relevant equations are $F = qE$, $F = qvB$, and $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi a}$; where $F$ is force, $q$ is charge, $v$ is speed, $I$ is current, and $a$ is distance.
questions.solution:
From the relation $F = qE$, the electric field $E$ is given by $E = \dfrac{F}{q}$.

The dimensional formula for force $F$ is $MLT^{-2}$.

The dimensional formula for charge $q$ is $IT$.

Substituting these dimensions, we get:

$[E] = \dfrac{MLT^{-2}}{IT} = MLT^{-3}I^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Determine the dimensions of torque $\Gamma$.
Some of the equations involving related quantities are
$\omega = \dfrac{\theta_2 - \theta_1}{t_2 - t_1}$, $\alpha = \dfrac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{t_2 - t_1}$, $\Gamma = F \cdot r$ and $I = mr^2$.
The symbols have standard meanings.
questions.solution:
Torque $\Gamma$ is given by the product of force $F$ and distance $r$.

$\Gamma = F \cdot r$

The dimensional formula of force $F$ is $[M L T^{-2}]$.

The dimensional formula of distance $r$ is $[L]$.

Substituting these dimensions into the formula for torque:

$[\Gamma] = [M L T^{-2}] \times [L] = [M L^2 T^{-2}]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the dimensions of linear momentum.
questions.solution:
Linear momentum is defined as the product of mass and velocity.

$p = m \times v$

The dimensional formula for mass is $[M]$.

The dimensional formula for velocity is $[LT^{-1}]$.

Substituting these dimensions, we get the dimensional formula for linear momentum:

$[p] = [M] \times [LT^{-1}] = [MLT^{-1}]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the dimensions of the magnetic field $B$. The relevant equations are $F = qE$, $F = qvB$, and $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi a}$; where $F$ is force, $q$ is charge, $v$ is speed, $I$ is current, and $a$ is distance.
questions.solution:
Using the formula for magnetic force $F = qvB$, the magnetic field $B$ is given by $B = \dfrac{F}{qv}$.

The dimensional formula for force $F$ is $[MLT^{-2}]$.

The dimensional formula for charge $q$ is $[IT]$.

The dimensional formula for velocity $v$ is $[LT^{-1}]$.

Substituting these dimensions into the expression for $B$, we get:

$[B] = \dfrac{[MLT^{-2}]}{[IT][LT^{-1}]}$

$[B] = \dfrac{[MLT^{-2}]}{[IL]}$

$[B] = [MT^{-2}I^{-1}]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the dimensions of the magnetic permeability $\mu_0$. The relevant equations are $F = qE$, $F = qvB$, and $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi a}$; where $F$ is force, $q$ is charge, $v$ is speed, $I$ is current, and $a$ is distance.
questions.solution:
From the given formula $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi a}$, we get $\mu_0 = \dfrac{2 \pi a B}{I}$.

From the magnetic force formula $F = qvB$, we get $B = \dfrac{F}{qv}$.

Substituting the expression for $B$ gives $\mu_0 = \dfrac{2 \pi a F}{qvI}$.

The dimensional formulas for the quantities are:

$[F] = MLT^{-2}$

$[a] = L$

$[q] = IT$

$[v] = LT^{-1}$

$[I] = I$

Substituting these dimensions into the expression for $\mu_0$:

$[\mu_0] = \dfrac{MLT^{-2} L}{(IT)(LT^{-1})I}$

$[\mu_0] = \dfrac{ML^2T^{-2}}{L I^2}$

$[\mu_0] = MLT^{-2}I^{-2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the dimensions of Planck's constant $h$ using the equation $E = h\nu$, where $E$ represents the energy and $\nu$ represents the frequency.
questions.solution:
Given equation is $E = h u$.

The dimensional formula for energy $E$ is $[ML^2 T^{-2}]$.

The dimensional formula for frequency $ u$ is $[T^{-1}]$.

Rearranging the equation for Planck's constant $h$, we get $h = \dfrac{E}{ u}$.

Substituting the dimensions, we get $[h] = \dfrac{[ML^2 T^{-2}]}{[T^{-1}]} = [ML^2 T^{-1}]$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find the dimensions of the specific heat capacity $c$. An equation involving this quantity is $Q = mc(T_2 - T_1)$.
questions.solution:
The given equation is $Q = mc(T_2 - T_1)$

Rearranging for specific heat capacity $c$:

$c = \dfrac{Q}{m(T_2 - T_1)}$

The dimensional formula for heat energy $Q$ is $[M L^2 T^{-2}]$

The dimensional formula for mass $m$ is $[M]$

The dimensional formula for temperature difference $(T_2 - T_1)$ is $[K]$

Substituting these dimensions:

$[c] = \dfrac{[M L^2 T^{-2}]}{[M][K]}$

$[c] = [L^2 T^{-2} K^{-1}]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find the dimensions of the gas constant $R$. An equation involving this quantity is $PV = nRT$.
questions.solution:
The ideal gas equation is given by $PV = nRT$.

Rearranging for the gas constant $R$, we get:

$R = \dfrac{PV}{nT}$

The dimensions of pressure $P$ (Force/Area) are $[M L^{-1} T^{-2}]$.

The dimensions of volume $V$ are $[L^3]$.

The dimensions of number of moles $n$ are $[\text{mol}]$ and temperature $T$ are $[K]$.

Substituting these dimensions into the equation for $R$, we get:

$[R] = \dfrac{[M L^{-1} T^{-2}][L^3]}{[\text{mol}][K]}$

$[R] = [M L^2 T^{-2} K^{-1} (\text{mol})^{-1}]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find the dimensions of the coefficient of linear expansion $\alpha$. An equation involving this quantity is $l_t = l_0[1 + \alpha(T_2 - T_1)]$.
questions.solution:
The given equation is $l_t = l_0[1 + \alpha(T_2 - T_1)]$.

Since $l_t$ and $l_0$ both represent lengths, their dimensions are $[L]$.

The quantity inside the square brackets, $1 + \alpha(T_2 - T_1)$, must be a dimensionless quantity because it is added to $1$.

Thus, the term $\alpha(T_2 - T_1)$ is dimensionless.

$[\alpha] [T_2 - T_1] = [M^0 L^0 T^0]$

Since $T_2 - T_1$ represents a change in temperature, its dimension is $[K]$.

$[\alpha] [K] = [M^0 L^0 T^0]$

$[\alpha] = [K^{-1}]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Assuming force, length, and time to be the fundamental quantities, determine the dimensions of density.
questions.solution:
Density is defined as mass per unit volume.

Density $= \dfrac{M}{V} = \dfrac{M}{L^3}$

From Newton's second law, force is the product of mass and acceleration.

$F = M \times A = M \times LT^{-2}$

$M = FL^{-1}T^2$

Substituting the dimensions of mass in the expression for density:

Density $= \dfrac{FL^{-1}T^2}{L^3} = FL^{-4}T^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Determine the dimensions of moment of inertia $I$.
Some of the equations involving related quantities are
$\omega = \dfrac{\theta_2 - \theta_1}{t_2 - t_1}$, $\alpha = \dfrac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{t_2 - t_1}$, $\Gamma = F \cdot r$ and $I = mr^2$.
The symbols have standard meanings.
questions.solution:
From the given equation, $I = mr^2$.

The dimensional formula for mass $m$ is $[M]$.

The dimensional formula for distance $r$ is $[L]$.

Substituting these dimensions, the dimensional formula for moment of inertia $I$ is $[M][L]^2 = [ML^2]$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Determine the dimensions of angular speed $\omega$.
Some of the equations involving related quantities are
$\omega = \dfrac{\theta_2 - \theta_1}{t_2 - t_1}$, $\alpha = \dfrac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{t_2 - t_1}$, $\Gamma = F \cdot r$ and $I = mr^2$.
The symbols have standard meanings.
questions.solution:
Angular speed $\omega$ is given by the relation $\omega = \dfrac{\theta_2 - \theta_1}{t_2 - t_1}$.

Angular displacement $\theta$ is the ratio of arc length to radius, making it a dimensionless quantity. Its dimensional formula is $M^0 L^0 T^0$.

Time $t$ has the dimensional formula $T$.

Substituting the dimensions into the equation for angular speed:

$[\omega] = \dfrac{M^0 L^0 T^0}{T} = T^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the dimensions of pressure.
questions.solution:
Pressure is defined as force per unit area.

The dimensional formula of force is $[MLT^{-2}]$.

The dimensional formula of area is $[L^2]$.

Therefore, the dimensional formula of pressure is $\dfrac{[MLT^{-2}]}{[L^2]} = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Assuming force, length, and time to be the fundamental quantities, determine the dimensions of momentum.
questions.solution:
The dimensional formula for momentum is $[p] = MLT^{-1}$.

The dimensional formula for force is $[F] = MLT^{-2}$.

We can express the dimensions of momentum as $[p] = (MLT^{-2}) \times T$.

Substituting the fundamental quantity force $F$ and time $T$, we get $[p] = FT$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Assuming force, length, and time to be the fundamental quantities, determine the dimensions of energy.
questions.solution:
Energy is defined as the capacity to do work, and work is the product of force and displacement.

Therefore, the dimensional formula for energy can be written as:

$[\text{Energy}] = [\text{Force}] \times [\text{Displacement}]$

Substituting the fundamental quantities given in the problem, where force is $F$ and length (displacement) is $L$:

$[\text{Energy}] = F \times L = FL$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Assume the acceleration due to gravity at a certain location is $10 \text{ m/s}^2$. Determine its value in $\text{cm/(minute)}^2$.
questions.solution:
Given $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.

To convert the unit from $\text{m/s}^2$ to $\text{cm/min}^2$, we use the conversions $1 \text{ m} = 100 \text{ cm}$ and $1 \text{ s} = \dfrac{1}{60} \text{ min}$.

Substituting these into the given value:

$g = 10 \times \dfrac{100 \text{ cm}}{\left(\dfrac{1}{60} \text{ min}\right)^2}$

$g = 10 \times 100 \times (60)^2 \text{ cm/min}^2$

$g = 1000 \times 3600 \text{ cm/min}^2$

$g = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ cm/min}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The average speed of a snail is $0.020$ miles/hour, whereas that of a leopard is $70$ miles/hour. Determine these speeds in SI units.
questions.solution:
The SI unit of speed is $\text{m/s}$.

We know that $1 \text{ mile} \approx 1609 \text{ m}$ and $1 \text{ hour} = 3600 \text{ s}$.

The conversion factor from $\text{miles/hour}$ to $\text{m/s}$ is $\dfrac{1609}{3600} \approx 0.447 \text{ m/s}$.

Speed of the snail $= 0.020 \text{ miles/hour} = 0.020 \times 0.447 \text{ m/s} \approx 0.0089 \text{ m/s}$.

Speed of the leopard $= 70 \text{ miles/hour} = 70 \times 0.447 \text{ m/s} \approx 31 \text{ m/s}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a Calcutta laboratory, the height of the mercury column in a barometer was recorded as $75 \text{ cm}$. Determine this pressure in SI and CGS units using the following data: specific gravity of mercury $= 13.6$, density of water $= 10^3 \text{ kg/m}^3$, and $g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$ at Calcutta. Use the formula Pressure $= h\rho g$ in usual symbols.
questions.solution:
Given $h = 75 \text{ cm} = 0.75 \text{ m}$

Density of mercury, $\rho = 13.6 \times 10^3 \text{ kg/m}^3$

Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$

In SI units, the pressure is given by:

$P = h\rho g = 0.75 \times 13.6 \times 10^3 \times 9.8$

$P = 99.96 \times 10^3 \text{ N/m}^2 \approx 10 \times 10^4 \text{ N/m}^2$

In CGS units:

$h = 75 \text{ cm}$

$\rho = 13.6 \text{ g/cm}^3$

$g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2 = 980 \text{ cm/s}^2$

$P = h\rho g = 75 \times 13.6 \times 980$

$P = 999600 \text{ dyne/cm}^2 \approx 10 \times 10^5 \text{ dyne/cm}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The surface tension of water is $72 \text{ dyne/cm}$. Convert this value into SI units.
questions.solution:
The given value of surface tension is $72 \text{ dyne/cm}$.

The SI unit of surface tension is $\text{N/m}$.

Using the conversion factors:
$1 \text{ dyne} = 10^{-5} \text{ N}$
$1 \text{ cm} = 10^{-2} \text{ m}$

Substituting these into the given value:
$72 \text{ dyne/cm} = 72 \times \dfrac{10^{-5} \text{ N}}{10^{-2} \text{ m}}$

$= 72 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N/m}$

$= 0.072 \text{ N/m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the power of a $100 \text{ watt}$ bulb in CGS units.
questions.solution:
Given power of the bulb is $P = 100 \text{ W}$.

The SI unit of power is watt (W), which is equal to joule per second (J/s).

Therefore, $P = 100 \text{ J/s}$.

In the CGS system, the unit of energy is erg, and $1 \text{ J} = 10^7 \text{ erg}$.

Converting the power into CGS units:

$P = 100 \times 10^7 \text{ erg/s} = 10^9 \text{ erg/s}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the dimensions of magnetic dipole moment $M$. The defining equation is $M = IA$, where $A$ is area and $I$ is current.
questions.solution:
The defining equation for magnetic dipole moment is $M = IA$.

The dimensional formula for current $I$ is $[I]$.

The dimensional formula for area $A$ is $[L^2]$.

Substituting these dimensions into the equation, we get:

$[M] = [I] \times [L^2] = [L^2I]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rotating body's kinetic energy $K$ relies on its moment of inertia $I$ and its angular speed $\omega$. Suppose the relation is $K = kI^a \omega^b$, where $k$ is a dimensionless constant. Determine $a$ and $b$. The moment of inertia of a sphere about its diameter is given as $\dfrac{2}{5} Mr^2$.
questions.solution:
Using dimensional analysis, the dimensions of the given quantities are:

$[K] = [M L^2 T^{-2}]$

$[I] = [M L^2]$

$[\omega] = [T^{-1}]$

Substituting these into the given relation $K = k I^a \omega^b$:

$[M L^2 T^{-2}] = [M L^2]^a [T^{-1}]^b$

$[M L^2 T^{-2}] = [M^a L^{2a} T^{-b}]$

Comparing the powers of $M$, $L$, and $T$ on both sides:

For $M$: $a = 1$

For $T$: $-b = -2 \Rightarrow b = 2$

Therefore, $a = 1$ and $b = 2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Assuming force, length, and time to be the fundamental quantities, determine the dimensions of pressure.
questions.solution:
Pressure is defined as force acting per unit area.

$P = \dfrac{F}{A}$

The dimensional formula for area is $L^2$.

Substituting the fundamental dimensions of force and area, we get:

$[P] = \dfrac{F}{L^2} = FL^{-2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the dimensions of electric dipole moment $p$. The defining equation is $p = q \cdot d$, where $d$ is distance and $q$ is charge.
questions.solution:
The defining equation for electric dipole moment is $p = q \cdot d$.

The dimensions of charge $q$ are given by current $\times$ time, which is $[I T]$.

The dimensions of distance $d$ are $[L]$.

Substituting these into the equation, we get $[p] = [I T] \cdot [L] = [L T I]$.

Thus, the dimensions of electric dipole moment are $LTI$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Let $I$ represent the current through a conductor, $R$ its resistance, and $V$ the potential difference across its ends. According to Ohm's law, the product of two of these quantities is equal to the third. Determine Ohm's law using dimensional analysis. The dimensional formulae for $R$ and $V$ are $ML^2 I^{-2} T^{-3}$ and $ML^2 T^{-3} I^{-1}$ respectively.
questions.solution:
The dimensional formula for potential difference $V$ is $[M L^2 T^{-3} I^{-1}]$.

The dimensional formula for resistance $R$ is $[M L^2 T^{-3} I^{-2}]$.

The dimensional formula for current $I$ is $[I]$.

Checking the dimensions of the product $IR$:

$[I][R] = [I] imes [M L^2 T^{-3} I^{-2}] = [M L^2 T^{-3} I^{-1}]$

This matches the dimensional formula for $V$.

Checking the other relations:

$[I][V] = [I] imes [M L^2 T^{-3} I^{-1}] = [M L^2 T^{-3}] eq [R]$

$[V][R] = [M L^2 T^{-3} I^{-1}] imes [M L^2 T^{-3} I^{-2}] = [M^2 L^4 T^{-6} I^{-3}] eq [I]$

$[I^2][R] = [I^2] imes [M L^2 T^{-3} I^{-2}] = [M L^2 T^{-3}] eq [V]$

Thus, the correct relation according to dimensional analysis is $V = IR$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The vibration frequency of a string depends on the length $L$ between the nodes, the tension $F$ in the string, and its mass per unit length $m$. Determine the expression for its frequency using dimensional analysis, assuming $k$ is a dimensionless constant.
questions.solution:
Let the frequency $f$ be given by $f = k L^a F^b m^c$.

Writing the dimensions of each physical quantity:
$[f] = [T^{-1}]$
$[L] = [L]$
$[F] = [M L T^{-2}]$
$[m] = [M L^{-1}]$

Substituting the dimensions into the assumed relation:
$[T^{-1}] = [L]^a [M L T^{-2}]^b [M L^{-1}]^c$
$[T^{-1}] = [M]^{b+c} [L]^{a+b-c} [T]^{-2b}$

Equating the powers of $M$, $L$, and $T$ on both sides:
$b + c = 0$
$a + b - c = 0$
$-2b = -1$

Solving these equations:
$b = \dfrac{1}{2}$
$c = -b = -\dfrac{1}{2}$
$a = c - b = -\dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{1}{2} = -1$

Substituting the values of $a$, $b$, and $c$ back into the expression:
$f = k L^{-1} F^{1/2} m^{-1/2} = \dfrac{k}{L} \sqrt{\dfrac{F}{m}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Determine if the following equation is dimensionally correct:
$V = \dfrac{\pi P r^4 t}{8 \eta l}$
where $h =$ height, $S =$ surface tension, $\rho =$ density, $P =$ pressure, $V =$ volume, $\eta =$ coefficient of viscosity, $\nu =$ frequency and $I =$ moment of inertia.
options: The equation is dimensionally incorrect, as the right side has dimensions of $[M L^2 T^{-2}]$.
The equation is dimensionally incorrect, as the right side has dimensions of $[L^2]$.
The equation is dimensionally incorrect, as the right side has dimensions of $[L^3 T^{-1}]$.
questions.solution:
The dimensions of the terms are evaluated as follows:

$[V] = [L^3]$

$[P] = [M L^{-1} T^{-2}]$

$[r] = [L]$

$[t] = [T]$

$[\eta] = [M L^{-1} T^{-1}]$

$[l] = [L]$

Substituting these dimensions into the right hand side of the given equation:

$\dfrac{[M L^{-1} T^{-2}] [L^4] [T]}{[M L^{-1} T^{-1}] [L]} = \dfrac{[M L^3 T^{-1}]}{[M L^0 T^{-1}]} = [L^3]$

Since the dimensions of the left hand side and right hand side are equal, the equation is dimensionally correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Let $x$ and $a$ represent distance. Is the equation $\displaystyle \int \dfrac{dx}{\sqrt{a^2 - x^2}} = \dfrac{1}{a} \sin^{-1}\dfrac{a}{x}$ dimensionally correct?
options: No, the left-hand side has dimensions of $[L]$ while the right-hand side is dimensionless.
No, the left-hand side is dimensionless while the right-hand side has dimensions of $[L^{-1}]$.
Yes, both sides have dimensions of $[L^{-1}]$.
questions.solution:
The dimension of $dx$ is $[L]$.

The dimension of $\sqrt{a^2 - x^2}$ is $\sqrt{[L^2]} = [L]$.

The dimension of the left-hand side is $\dfrac{[L]}{[L]} = [M^0 L^0 T^0]$, which is dimensionless.

On the right-hand side, the argument of the inverse sine function, $\dfrac{a}{x}$, is dimensionless. The inverse sine function itself represents an angle, which is also dimensionless.

The term $\dfrac{1}{a}$ has the dimension $[L^{-1}]$.

Therefore, the dimension of the right-hand side is $[L^{-1}]$.

Since the left-hand side is dimensionless and the right-hand side has dimensions of $[L^{-1}]$, the equation is not dimensionally correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The normal duration of an I.Sc. Physics practical period in Indian colleges is $100$ minutes. Determine this period in microcenturies, given that $1 \text{ microcentury} = 10^{-6} \times 100 \text{ years}$.
questions.solution:
$1 \text{ microcentury} = 10^{-6} \times 100 \text{ years} = 10^{-4} \text{ years}$

$1 \text{ year} = 365 \text{ days} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 \text{ minutes} = 525600 \text{ minutes}$

$1 \text{ microcentury} = 10^{-4} \times 525600 \text{ minutes} = 52.56 \text{ minutes}$

$\text{Duration of the period} = 100 \text{ minutes}$

$\text{Duration in microcenturies} = \dfrac{100}{52.56} \approx 1.9 \text{ microcenturies}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: According to the theory of relativity, mass can be transformed into energy. The resulting energy $E$ is proportional to certain powers of the mass $m$ and the speed of light $c$. Determine a relation among these quantities by applying the method of dimensions.
questions.solution:
Let $E = k m^a c^b$, where $k$ is a dimensionless constant.

Writing the dimensional formula of each physical quantity:

$[E] = [M L^2 T^{-2}]$

$[m] = [M]$

$[c] = [L T^{-1}]$

Substituting the dimensions in the assumed relation:

$[M L^2 T^{-2}] = [M]^a [L T^{-1}]^b$

$[M L^2 T^{-2}] = [M^a L^b T^{-b}]$

Equating the powers of $M$, $L$, and $T$ on both sides:

$a = 1$

$b = 2$

$-b = -2 \Rightarrow b = 2$

Substituting the values of $a$ and $b$ in the assumed relation:

$E = k m^1 c^2$

$E = k m c^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Determine if the following equation is dimensionally correct:
$h = \dfrac{2 S \cos\theta}{\rho r g}$
where $h =$ height, $S =$ surface tension, $\rho =$ density, $P =$ pressure, $V =$ volume, $\eta =$ coefficient of viscosity, $\nu =$ frequency and $I =$ moment of inertia.
options: The equation is dimensionally incorrect, as the right side has dimensions of $[L^2]$.
The equation is dimensionally incorrect, as the right side has dimensions of $[L^{-1}]$.
The equation is dimensionally incorrect, as the right side has dimensions of $[M L T^{-2}]$.
questions.solution:
Dimensions of the left-hand side:

$[h] = [L]$

Dimensions of the quantities on the right-hand side:

$[S] = [M T^{-2}]$

$[\cos\theta] = [M^0 L^0 T^0]$

$[\rho] = [M L^{-3}]$

$[r] = [L]$

$[g] = [L T^{-2}]$

Substituting these dimensions into the right-hand side:

RHS $= \dfrac{[M T^{-2}]}{[M L^{-3}] [L] [L T^{-2}]}$

RHS $= \dfrac{[M T^{-2}]}{[M L^{-1} T^{-2}]}$

RHS $= [L]$

Since the dimensions of the left-hand side equal the dimensions of the right-hand side, the equation is dimensionally correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Determine if the following equation is dimensionally correct:
$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\rho}}$
where $h =$ height, $S =$ surface tension, $\rho =$ density, $P =$ pressure, $V =$ volume, $\eta =$ coefficient of viscosity, $\nu =$ frequency and $I =$ moment of inertia.
options: The equation is dimensionally incorrect, as the right side has dimensions of $[L T^{-2}]$.
The equation is dimensionally incorrect, as the right side has dimensions of $[L^2 T^{-2}]$.
The equation is dimensionally incorrect, as the right side has dimensions of $[M L T^{-1}]$.
questions.solution:
The dimensions of the left-hand side (velocity, $v$) are:
$[v] = [L T^{-1}]$

The dimensions of the terms on the right-hand side are:
Pressure, $P = \dfrac{\text{Force}}{\text{Area}} = \dfrac{[M L T^{-2}]}{[L^2]} = [M L^{-1} T^{-2}]$
Density, $\rho = \dfrac{\text{Mass}}{\text{Volume}} = \dfrac{[M]}{[L^3]} = [M L^{-3}]$

Substituting these into the right-hand side expression:
$\sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{[M L^{-1} T^{-2}]}{[M L^{-3}]}} = \sqrt{[L^2 T^{-2}]} = [L T^{-1}]$

Since the dimensions of the left-hand side are equal to the dimensions of the right-hand side, the equation is dimensionally correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Determine if the following equation is dimensionally correct:
$\nu = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{mgl}{I}}$
where $h =$ height, $S =$ surface tension, $\rho =$ density, $P =$ pressure, $V =$ volume, $\eta =$ coefficient of viscosity, $\nu =$ frequency and $I =$ moment of inertia.
options: The equation is dimensionally incorrect, as the right side has dimensions of $[T^{-2}]$.
The equation is dimensionally incorrect, as the right side is dimensionless.
The equation is dimensionally incorrect, as the right side has dimensions of $[M L^2 T^{-1}]$.
questions.solution:
Dimensions of the left hand side (LHS):
$[\nu] = [T^{-1}]$

Dimensions of the terms on the right hand side (RHS):
$[m] = [M]$
$[g] = [L T^{-2}]$
$[l] = [L]$
$[I] = [M L^2]$

Substituting these into the expression under the square root:
$\left[\dfrac{mgl}{I}\right] = \dfrac{[M][L T^{-2}][L]}{[M L^2]} = [T^{-2}]$

Taking the square root for the RHS:
$\left[\sqrt{\dfrac{mgl}{I}}\right] = \sqrt{[T^{-2}]} = [T^{-1}]$

Since the dimensions of the LHS and RHS are equal, the equation is dimensionally correct.
